title: Neurology

infobox.value: [Neuropathy](/wiki/Neuropathy "Neuropathy"), [dementia](/wiki/Dementia "Dementia"), [stroke](/wiki/Stroke "Stroke"), [encephalopathy](/wiki/Encephalopathy "Encephalopathy"), [Parkinson's disease](/wiki/Parkinson%27s_disease "Parkinson's disease"), [epilepsy](/wiki/Epilepsy "Epilepsy"), [meningitis](/wiki/Meningitis "Meningitis"), [muscular dystrophy](/wiki/Muscular_dystrophy "Muscular dystrophy"), [migraine](/wiki/Migraine "Migraine"), [attention deficit/hyperactivity disorder](/wiki/Attention_deficit/hyperactivity_disorder "Attention deficit/hyperactivity disorder"), [narcolepsy](/wiki/Narcolepsy "Narcolepsy")

[Computed axial tomography](/wiki/Computed_axial_tomography "Computed axial tomography"), [MRI scan](/wiki/MRI_scan "MRI scan"), [lumbar puncture](/wiki/Lumbar_puncture "Lumbar puncture"), [electroencephalography](/wiki/Electroencephalography "Electroencephalography")

[M.D.](/wiki/Doctor_of_Medicine "Doctor of Medicine") or [D.O.](/wiki/Doctor_of_Osteopathic_Medicine "Doctor of Osteopathic Medicine") (US), [M.B.B.S.](/wiki/M.B.B.S. "M.B.B.S.") (UK), D.M. (Doctorate of Medicine) (India), M.B. B.Ch. B.A.O. (Republic of Ireland)[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

(from [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language"): [νεῦρον (neûron)](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%CE%BD%CE%B5%E1%BF%A6%CF%81%CE%BF%CE%BD "wikt:νεῦρον"), "string, nerve" and the suffix [-logia](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/-logia "wikt:-logia"), "study of") is the branch of [medicine](/wiki/Specialty_(medicine) "Specialty (medicine)") dealing with the diagnosis and treatment of all categories of conditions and disease involving the [nervous system](/wiki/Nervous_system "Nervous system"), which comprises the [brain](/wiki/Human_brain "Human brain"), the [spinal cord](/wiki/Spinal_cord "Spinal cord") and the  [peripheral nerves](/wiki/Peripheral_nervous_system "Peripheral nervous system").[[1]](#cite_note-1) Neurological practice relies heavily on the field of [neuroscience](/wiki/Neuroscience "Neuroscience"), the scientific study of the [nervous system](/wiki/Nervous_system "Nervous system"), using various techniques of [neurotherapy](/wiki/Neurotherapy "Neurotherapy").[[2]](#cite_note-EEE_Brain_2019-2)[[3]](#cite_note-International_Neuromodulation_Society_2024-3)[[4]](#cite_note-Val_Danilov_Origin_Neurostimulation_2024-4)

is a [physician](/wiki/Physician "Physician") specializing in neurology and trained to investigate, diagnose and treat [neurological disorders](/wiki/Neurological_disorder "Neurological disorder").[[5]](#cite_note-WorkWYourDoc-5) Neurologists diagnose and treat myriad neurologic conditions, including [stroke](/wiki/Stroke "Stroke"), [epilepsy](/wiki/Epilepsy "Epilepsy"), [movement disorders](/wiki/Movement_disorder "Movement disorder") such as [Parkinson's disease](/wiki/Parkinson%27s_disease "Parkinson's disease"), [brain infections](/wiki/List_of_infections_of_the_central_nervous_system "List of infections of the central nervous system"), autoimmune neurologic disorders such as [multiple sclerosis](/wiki/Multiple_sclerosis "Multiple sclerosis"), [sleep disorders](/wiki/Sleep_disorder "Sleep disorder"), brain injury, headache disorders like [migraine](/wiki/Migraine "Migraine"), [tumors of the brain](/wiki/Brain_tumor "Brain tumor") and [dementias](/wiki/Dementia "Dementia") such as [Alzheimer's disease](/wiki/Alzheimer%27s_disease "Alzheimer's disease").[[6]](#cite_note-6) Neurologists may also have roles in [clinical research](/wiki/Clinical_research "Clinical research"), [clinical trials](/wiki/Clinical_trial "Clinical trial"), and [basic](/wiki/Basic_research "Basic research") or [translational research](/wiki/Translational_research "Translational research"). Neurology is a nonsurgical specialty, its corresponding [surgical](/wiki/Surgical "Surgical") specialty is [neurosurgery](/wiki/Neurosurgery "Neurosurgery").[[5]](#cite_note-WorkWYourDoc-5)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Medical specialty dealing with disorders of the nervous system

This article is about the branch of medicine. For the scientific study of the nervous system, see [Neuroscience](/wiki/Neuroscience "Neuroscience"). For the journal, see [*Neurology* (journal)](/wiki/Neurology_(journal) "Neurology (journal)").

"Neurological sciences" redirects here. For the journal, see [*Neurological Sciences* (journal)](/wiki/Neurological_Sciences_(journal) "Neurological Sciences (journal)").

**Neurology** (from [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language"): [νεῦρον (neûron)](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%CE%BD%CE%B5%E1%BF%A6%CF%81%CE%BF%CE%BD "wikt:νεῦρον"), "string, nerve" and the suffix [-logia](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/-logia "wikt:-logia"), "study of") is the branch of [medicine](/wiki/Specialty_(medicine) "Specialty (medicine)") dealing with the diagnosis and treatment of all categories of conditions and disease involving the [nervous system](/wiki/Nervous_system "Nervous system"), which comprises the [brain](/wiki/Human_brain "Human brain"), the [spinal cord](/wiki/Spinal_cord "Spinal cord") and the  [peripheral nerves](/wiki/Peripheral_nervous_system "Peripheral nervous system").[[1]](#cite_note-1) Neurological practice relies heavily on the field of [neuroscience](/wiki/Neuroscience "Neuroscience"), the scientific study of the [nervous system](/wiki/Nervous_system "Nervous system"), using various techniques of [neurotherapy](/wiki/Neurotherapy "Neurotherapy").[[2]](#cite_note-EEE_Brain_2019-2)[[3]](#cite_note-International_Neuromodulation_Society_2024-3)[[4]](#cite_note-Val_Danilov_Origin_Neurostimulation_2024-4)

A **neurologist** is a [physician](/wiki/Physician "Physician") specializing in neurology and trained to investigate, diagnose and treat [neurological disorders](/wiki/Neurological_disorder "Neurological disorder").[[5]](#cite_note-WorkWYourDoc-5) Neurologists diagnose and treat myriad neurologic conditions, including [stroke](/wiki/Stroke "Stroke"), [epilepsy](/wiki/Epilepsy "Epilepsy"), [movement disorders](/wiki/Movement_disorder "Movement disorder") such as [Parkinson's disease](/wiki/Parkinson%27s_disease "Parkinson's disease"), [brain infections](/wiki/List_of_infections_of_the_central_nervous_system "List of infections of the central nervous system"), autoimmune neurologic disorders such as [multiple sclerosis](/wiki/Multiple_sclerosis "Multiple sclerosis"), [sleep disorders](/wiki/Sleep_disorder "Sleep disorder"), brain injury, headache disorders like [migraine](/wiki/Migraine "Migraine"), [tumors of the brain](/wiki/Brain_tumor "Brain tumor") and [dementias](/wiki/Dementia "Dementia") such as [Alzheimer's disease](/wiki/Alzheimer%27s_disease "Alzheimer's disease").[[6]](#cite_note-6) Neurologists may also have roles in [clinical research](/wiki/Clinical_research "Clinical research"), [clinical trials](/wiki/Clinical_trial "Clinical trial"), and [basic](/wiki/Basic_research "Basic research") or [translational research](/wiki/Translational_research "Translational research"). Neurology is a nonsurgical specialty, its corresponding [surgical](/wiki/Surgical "Surgical") specialty is [neurosurgery](/wiki/Neurosurgery "Neurosurgery").[[5]](#cite_note-WorkWYourDoc-5)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neurology&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of neurology](/wiki/History_of_neurology "History of neurology")

The academic discipline began between the 15th and 16th centuries with the work and research of many neurologists such as [Thomas Willis](/wiki/Thomas_Willis "Thomas Willis"), [Robert Whytt](/wiki/Robert_Whytt "Robert Whytt"), [Matthew Baillie](/wiki/Matthew_Baillie "Matthew Baillie"), [Charles Bell](/wiki/Charles_Bell "Charles Bell"), [Moritz Heinrich Romberg](/wiki/Moritz_Heinrich_Romberg "Moritz Heinrich Romberg"), [Duchenne de Boulogne](/wiki/Duchenne_de_Boulogne "Duchenne de Boulogne"), [William A. Hammond](/wiki/William_A._Hammond "William A. Hammond"), [Jean-Martin Charcot](/wiki/Jean-Martin_Charcot "Jean-Martin Charcot"), [C. Miller Fisher](/wiki/C._Miller_Fisher "C. Miller Fisher") and [John Hughlings Jackson](/wiki/John_Hughlings_Jackson "John Hughlings Jackson"). Neo-Latin *neurologia* appeared in various texts from 1610 denoting an anatomical focus on the nerves (variably understood as vessels), and was most notably used by Willis, who preferred Greek νευρολογία.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

"M.B.B.S.") (UK), D.M. (Doctorate of Medicine) (India), M.B. B.Ch. B.A.O. (Republic of Ireland)[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Edward_Flatau.jpg/250px-Edward_Flatau.jpg)](/wiki/File:Edward_Flatau.jpg)

[Polish](/wiki/Polish_people "Polish people") neurologist [Edward Flatau](/wiki/Edward_Flatau "Edward Flatau") greatly influenced the developing field of neurology. He published a human brain atlas in 1894 and wrote a fundamental book on migraines in 1912.

 [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Charcot1893.jpg/250px-Charcot1893.jpg)](/wiki/File:Charcot1893.jpg)

[Jean-Martin Charcot](/wiki/Jean-Martin_Charcot "Jean-Martin Charcot") is considered one of the fathers of neurology.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

In the United States and Canada, neurologists are physicians who have completed a postgraduate training period known as [residency](/wiki/Residency_(medicine) "Residency (medicine)") specializing in neurology after graduation from [medical school](/wiki/Medical_school "Medical school"). This additional training period typically lasts four years, with the first year devoted to training in [internal medicine](/wiki/Internal_medicine "Internal medicine").[[12]](#cite_note-12) On average, neurologists complete a total of eight to ten years of training. This includes four years of medical school, four years of residency and an optional one to two years of fellowship.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

While neurologists may treat general neurologic conditions, some neurologists go on to receive additional training focusing on a particular subspecialty in the field of neurology. These training programs are called [fellowships](/wiki/Fellowship_(medicine) "Fellowship (medicine)"), and are one to three years in duration. Subspecialties in the United States include brain injury medicine, [clinical neurophysiology](/wiki/Clinical_neurophysiology "Clinical neurophysiology"), [epilepsy](/wiki/Epilepsy "Epilepsy"), neurodevelopmental disabilities, [neuromuscular medicine](/wiki/Neuromuscular_medicine "Neuromuscular medicine"), [pain medicine](/wiki/Pain_medicine "Pain medicine"), [sleep medicine](/wiki/Sleep_medicine "Sleep medicine"), neurocritical care, vascular neurology (stroke),[[14]](#cite_note-14) [behavioral neurology](/wiki/Behavioral_neurology "Behavioral neurology"), headache, neuroimmunology[[15]](#cite_note-15) and infectious disease, [movement disorders](/wiki/Movement_disorder "Movement disorder"), neuroimaging, neurooncology, and neurorehabilitation.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

In Germany, a compulsory year of psychiatry must be done to complete a residency of neurology.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

In the United Kingdom and Ireland, neurology is a subspecialty of general (internal) medicine. After five years of medical school and two years as a Foundation Trainee, an aspiring neurologist must pass the examination for [Membership of the Royal College of Physicians](/wiki/Membership_of_the_Royal_College_of_Physicians "Membership of the Royal College of Physicians") (or the Irish equivalent) and complete two years of core medical training before entering specialist training in neurology.[[18]](#cite_note-18) Up to the 1960s, some intending to become neurologists would also spend two years working in psychiatric units before obtaining a diploma in psychological medicine. However, that was uncommon and, now that the [MRCPsych](/wiki/MRCPsych "MRCPsych") takes three years to obtain, would no longer be practical. A period of research is essential, and obtaining a higher degree aids career progression. Many found it was eased after an attachment to the Institute of Neurology at [Queen Square](/wiki/Queen_Square,_London "Queen Square, London"), London. Some neurologists enter the field of rehabilitation medicine (known as [physiatry](/wiki/Physiatry "Physiatry") in the US) to specialise in neurological rehabilitation, which may include stroke medicine, as well as traumatic brain injuries.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

## Physical examination

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neurology&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Physical examination")]

Main article: [Neurological examination](/wiki/Neurological_examination "Neurological examination")

During a [neurological examination](/wiki/Neurological_examination "Neurological examination"), the neurologist reviews the patient's health history with special attention to the patient's neurologic complaints. The patient then takes a neurological exam. Typically, the exam tests mental status, function of the [cranial nerves](/wiki/Cranial_nerves "Cranial nerves") (including vision), strength, coordination, reflexes, sensation and gait. This information helps the neurologist determine whether the problem exists in the nervous system and the clinical localization. Localization of the pathology is the key process by which neurologists develop their differential diagnosis. Further tests may be needed to confirm a diagnosis and ultimately guide therapy and appropriate management. Useful adjunct imaging studies in neurology include [CT scanning](/wiki/CT_scan "CT scan") and [magnetic resonance imaging](/wiki/Magnetic_resonance_imaging "Magnetic resonance imaging") (MRI). Other tests used to assess muscle and nerve function include [nerve conduction studies](/wiki/Nerve_conduction_studies "Nerve conduction studies") and [electromyography](/wiki/Electromyography "Electromyography").[[20]](#cite_note-20)

## Clinical tasks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neurology&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Clinical tasks")]

Neurologists examine patients who are referred to them by other physicians in both the [inpatient](/wiki/Inpatient "Inpatient") and [outpatient](/wiki/Outpatient "Outpatient") settings. Neurologists begin their interactions with patients by taking a comprehensive [medical history](/wiki/Medical_history "Medical history"), and then performing a [physical examination](/wiki/Physical_examination "Physical examination") focusing on evaluating the nervous system. Components of the [neurological examination](/wiki/Neurological_examination "Neurological examination") include assessment of the patient's [cognitive function](/wiki/Cognitive_function "Cognitive function"), [cranial nerves](/wiki/Cranial_nerves "Cranial nerves"), motor strength, [sensation](/wiki/Sensory_nervous_system "Sensory nervous system"), [reflexes](/wiki/Reflexes "Reflexes"), [coordination](/wiki/Motor_coordination "Motor coordination"), and [gait](/wiki/Gait "Gait").[[21]](#cite_note-21)

In some instances, neurologists may order additional [diagnostic tests](/wiki/Diagnostic_tests "Diagnostic tests") as part of the evaluation. Commonly employed tests in neurology include imaging studies such as [computed axial tomography](/wiki/Computed_axial_tomography "Computed axial tomography") (CAT) scans, [magnetic resonance imaging](/wiki/Magnetic_resonance_imaging "Magnetic resonance imaging") (MRI), and [ultrasound](/wiki/Ultrasound "Ultrasound") of major blood vessels of the head and neck. Neurophysiologic studies, including [electroencephalography](/wiki/Electroencephalography "Electroencephalography") (EEG), needle [electromyography](/wiki/Electromyography "Electromyography") (EMG), [nerve conduction studies](/wiki/Nerve_conduction_studies "Nerve conduction studies") (NCSs) and [evoked potentials](/wiki/Evoked_potentials "Evoked potentials") are also commonly ordered.[[22]](#cite_note-22) Neurologists frequently perform [lumbar punctures](/wiki/Lumbar_puncture "Lumbar puncture") to assess characteristics of a patient's [cerebrospinal fluid](/wiki/Cerebrospinal_fluid "Cerebrospinal fluid").[[23]](#cite_note-23) Advances in [genetic testing](/wiki/Genetic_testing "Genetic testing") have made genetic testing an important tool in the classification of inherited neuromuscular disease and diagnosis of many other neurogenetic diseases. The role of genetic influences on the development of acquired neurologic diseases is an active area of research.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

### Conditions and treatments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neurology&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Conditions and treatments")]

Some of the commonly encountered conditions treated by neurologists include headaches, [radiculopathy](/wiki/Radiculopathy "Radiculopathy"), [neuropathy](/wiki/Neuropathy "Neuropathy"), stroke, [dementia](/wiki/Dementia "Dementia"), [seizures](/wiki/Seizure "Seizure") and [epilepsy](/wiki/Epilepsy "Epilepsy"), [Alzheimer's disease](/wiki/Alzheimer%27s_disease "Alzheimer's disease"), [attention deficit/hyperactivity disorder](/wiki/Attention_deficit/hyperactivity_disorder "Attention deficit/hyperactivity disorder"), [Parkinson's disease](/wiki/Parkinson%27s_disease "Parkinson's disease"), [Tourette's syndrome](/wiki/Tourette%27s_syndrome "Tourette's syndrome"), [multiple sclerosis](/wiki/Multiple_sclerosis "Multiple sclerosis"), [head trauma](/wiki/Head_trauma "Head trauma"), [sleep disorders](/wiki/Sleep_disorder "Sleep disorder"), [neuromuscular diseases](/wiki/Neuromuscular_disease "Neuromuscular disease"), and various infections and tumors of the nervous system.[[25]](#cite_note-25) Neurologists are also asked to evaluate unresponsive patients on [life support](/wiki/Life_support "Life support") to confirm [brain death](/wiki/Brain_death "Brain death").[[26]](#cite_note-Wijdicks_Page_131-26)

Treatment options vary depending on the neurological problem. They can include referring the patient to a [physiotherapist](/wiki/Physiotherapist "Physiotherapist"), prescribing medications, or recommending a surgical procedure.

Some neurologists specialize in certain parts of the nervous system or in specific procedures. For example, clinical neurophysiologists specialize in the use of EEG and [intraoperative monitoring](/wiki/Intraoperative_neurophysiological_monitoring "Intraoperative neurophysiological monitoring") to diagnose certain neurological disorders.[[27]](#cite_note-27) Other neurologists specialize in the use of [electrodiagnostic medicine](/wiki/Electrodiagnostic_medicine "Electrodiagnostic medicine") studies – needle EMG and NCSs. In the US, physicians do not typically specialize in all the aspects of clinical neurophysiology – i.e. sleep, EEG, EMG, and NCSs. The American Board of Clinical Neurophysiology certifies US physicians in general clinical neurophysiology, epilepsy, and intraoperative monitoring.[[28]](#cite_note-28) The American Board of Electrodiagnostic Medicine certifies US physicians in [electrodiagnostic medicine](/wiki/Electrodiagnostic_medicine "Electrodiagnostic medicine") and certifies technologists in nerve-conduction studies. Sleep medicine is a subspecialty field in the US under several medical specialties including [anesthesiology](/wiki/Anesthesiology "Anesthesiology"), [internal medicine](/wiki/Internal_medicine "Internal medicine"), [family medicine](/wiki/Family_medicine "Family medicine"), and neurology.[[29]](#cite_note-29) Neurosurgery is a distinct specialty that involves a different training path and emphasizes the surgical treatment of neurological disorders.

Also, many nonmedical doctors, those with doctoral degrees (usually PhDs) in subjects such as biology and chemistry, study and research the nervous system. Working in laboratories in universities, hospitals, and private companies, these neuroscientists perform clinical and laboratory experiments and tests to learn more about the nervous system and find cures or new treatments for diseases and disorders.

A great deal of overlap occurs between [neuroscience](/wiki/Neuroscience "Neuroscience") and neurology. Many neurologists work in academic training hospitals, where they conduct research as neuroscientists in addition to treating patients and teaching neurology to [medical students](/wiki/Medical_student "Medical student").

[Neurotherapy](/wiki/Neurotherapy "Neurotherapy") involves systemic targeted delivery of an energy stimulus to a specific neurological zone or methods that retrain how the brain functions.[[2]](#cite_note-EEE_Brain_2019-2)[[3]](#cite_note-International_Neuromodulation_Society_2024-3)[[4]](#cite_note-Val_Danilov_Origin_Neurostimulation_2024-4)

### General responsibilities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neurology&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: General responsibilities")]

Neurologists are responsible for the diagnosis, treatment, and management of all the conditions mentioned above. When surgical or endovascular intervention is required, the neurologist may refer the patient to a [neurosurgeon](/wiki/Neurosurgeon "Neurosurgeon") or an [interventional neuroradiologist](/wiki/Interventional_neuroradiology "Interventional neuroradiology"). Neurologists frequently care for people with hereditary ([genetic](/wiki/Genetics "Genetics")) diseases when the major manifestations are neurological, as is frequently the case. [Lumbar punctures](/wiki/Lumbar_puncture "Lumbar puncture") are frequently performed by [neurologists](/wiki/Neurologists "Neurologists"). Some neurologists may develop an interest in particular subfields, such as stroke, [dementia](/wiki/Dementia "Dementia"), [movement disorders](/wiki/Movement_disorders "Movement disorders"), [neurointensive care](/wiki/Neurointensive_care "Neurointensive care"), headaches, [epilepsy](/wiki/Epilepsy "Epilepsy"), [sleep disorders](/wiki/Sleep_disorders "Sleep disorders"), chronic [pain](/wiki/Pain "Pain") management, [multiple sclerosis](/wiki/Multiple_sclerosis "Multiple sclerosis"), or [neuromuscular](/wiki/Neuromuscular "Neuromuscular") diseases.

### Overlapping areas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neurology&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Overlapping areas")]

Some overlap also occurs with other specialties, varying from country to country and even within a local geographic area. Acute [head trauma](/wiki/Traumatic_brain_injury "Traumatic brain injury") is most often treated by neurosurgeons, whereas [sequelae](/wiki/Sequelae "Sequelae") of head trauma may be treated by neurologists or [specialists in rehabilitation medicine](/wiki/Physical_medicine_and_rehabilitation "Physical medicine and rehabilitation"). Although stroke cases have been traditionally managed by internal medicine or hospitalists, the emergence of vascular neurology and [interventional neuroradiology](/wiki/Interventional_neuroradiology "Interventional neuroradiology") has created a demand for stroke specialists. The establishment of [Joint Commission](/wiki/Joint_Commission "Joint Commission")-certified stroke centres has increased the role of neurologists in stroke care in many primary, as well as tertiary, hospitals. Some cases of nervous system [infectious diseases](/wiki/Infectious_disease "Infectious disease") are treated by infectious disease specialists. Most cases of headache are diagnosed and treated primarily by [general practitioners](/wiki/General_practitioner "General practitioner"), at least the less severe cases. Likewise, most cases of [sciatica](/wiki/Sciatica "Sciatica") are treated by general practitioners, though they may be referred to neurologists or surgeons (neurosurgeons or [orthopedic surgeons](/wiki/Orthopedic_surgery "Orthopedic surgery")). [Sleep disorders](/wiki/Sleep_disorders "Sleep disorders") are also treated by [pulmonologists](/wiki/Pulmonology "Pulmonology") and [psychiatrists](/wiki/Psychiatrists "Psychiatrists"). [Cerebral palsy](/wiki/Cerebral_palsy "Cerebral palsy") is initially treated by [pediatricians](/wiki/Pediatrics "Pediatrics"), but care may be transferred to an adult neurologist after the patient reaches a certain age. [Physical medicine and rehabilitation](/wiki/Physical_medicine_and_rehabilitation "Physical medicine and rehabilitation") physicians may treat patients with neuromuscular diseases with electrodiagnostic studies (needle EMG and nerve-conduction studies) and other diagnostic tools. In the United Kingdom and other countries, many of the conditions encountered by older patients such as movement disorders, including Parkinson's disease, stroke, dementia, or gait disorders, are managed predominantly by specialists in [geriatric medicine](/wiki/Geriatric_medicine "Geriatric medicine").

[Clinical neuropsychologists](/wiki/Clinical_neuropsychology "Clinical neuropsychology") are often called upon to [evaluate](/wiki/Neuropsychological_assessment "Neuropsychological assessment") brain-[behavior](/wiki/Human_behavior "Human behavior") relationships for the purpose of assisting with [differential diagnosis](/wiki/Differential_diagnosis "Differential diagnosis"), planning [rehabilitation](/wiki/Physical_medicine_and_rehabilitation "Physical medicine and rehabilitation") strategies, documenting [cognitive](/wiki/Cognitive "Cognitive") strengths and weaknesses, and measuring change over time (e.g., for identifying abnormal [aging](/wiki/Aging "Aging") or tracking the progression of a [dementia](/wiki/Dementia "Dementia")).

### Relationship to clinical neurophysiology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neurology&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Relationship to clinical neurophysiology")]

In some countries such as the United States and Germany, [neurologists](/wiki/Neurologists "Neurologists") may subspecialize in [clinical neurophysiology](/wiki/Clinical_neurophysiology "Clinical neurophysiology"), the field responsible for EEG and [intraoperative monitoring](/wiki/Intraoperative_neurophysiological_monitoring "Intraoperative neurophysiological monitoring"), or in electrodiagnostic medicine [nerve conduction studies](/wiki/Nerve_conduction_study "Nerve conduction study"), EMG, and [evoked potentials](/wiki/Evoked_potentials "Evoked potentials"). In other countries, this is an autonomous [specialty](/wiki/Specialty_(medicine) "Specialty (medicine)") (e.g., United Kingdom, Sweden, Spain).

### Overlap with psychiatry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neurology&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Overlap with psychiatry")]

Further information: [Psychoneuroimmunology](/wiki/Psychoneuroimmunology "Psychoneuroimmunology") and [Neuropsychiatry](/wiki/Neuropsychiatry "Neuropsychiatry")

In the past, prior to the advent of more advanced diagnostic techniques such as [MRI](/wiki/MRI "MRI") some neurologists have considered psychiatry and neurology to overlap. Although modern physicians accept that brain chemistry plays a role in [mental illness](/wiki/Mental_illness "Mental illness"), traditionally they are classified separately from neurological disorders, and treated by [psychiatrists](/wiki/Psychiatrists "Psychiatrists"). In a 2002 review article in the *[American Journal of Psychiatry](/wiki/American_Journal_of_Psychiatry "American Journal of Psychiatry")*, Professor Joseph B. Martin, Dean of [Harvard Medical School](/wiki/Harvard_Medical_School "Harvard Medical School") and a neurologist by training, wrote, "the separation of the two categories is arbitrary, often influenced by beliefs rather than proven scientific observations. And the fact that the brain and mind are one makes the separation artificial anyway".[[30]](#cite_note-pmid11986119-30)

[Neurological disorders](/wiki/Neurological_disorders "Neurological disorders") often have [psychiatric](/wiki/Psychiatric "Psychiatric") manifestations, such as post-stroke depression, depression and [dementia](/wiki/Dementia "Dementia") associated with [Parkinson's disease](/wiki/Parkinson%27s_disease "Parkinson's disease"), mood and cognitive dysfunctions in Alzheimer's disease, and [Huntington disease](/wiki/Huntington_disease "Huntington disease"), to name a few. Hence, the sharp distinction between neurology and psychiatry is not always on a biological basis. The dominance of [psychoanalytic theory](/wiki/Psychoanalysis "Psychoanalysis") in the first three-quarters of the 20th century has since then been largely replaced by a focus on pharmacology.[[31]](#cite_note-31) Despite the shift to a medical [model](/wiki/Conceptual_model "Conceptual model"), brain science has not advanced to a point where scientists or [clinicians](/wiki/Clinicians "Clinicians") can point to readily discernible pathological lesions or genetic abnormalities that in and of themselves serve as reliable or predictive [biomarkers](/wiki/Biomarkers "Biomarkers") of a given mental disorder.

## Neurological enhancement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neurology&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Neurological enhancement")]

The emerging field of neurological enhancement highlights the potential of therapies to improve such things as workplace efficacy, attention in school, and overall happiness in personal lives.[[32]](#cite_note-32) However, this field has also given rise to questions about [neuroethics](/wiki/Neuroethics "Neuroethics").
